label: API Design
summary: API shapes, contracts, and surface-area design smells.

rules.label: Rust Arc Mutex Option State
rules.description: Arc<Mutex<Option<T>>>-style state bags that hide lifecycle state behind nested mutation layers.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Arc Mutex Option State matters because the pattern here, Arc<Mutex<Option<T>>>-style state bags that hide lifecycle state behind nested mutation layers, is worth review because good ownership and type contracts are a large part of what keeps Rust code ergonomic and safe.
rules.fix: For Rust Arc Mutex Option State in Rust, use the type system to make the contract obvious, and avoid APIs that force callers to remember hidden modes or invalid states.

rules.label: Rust Boolean State Machine
rules.description: State structs encoded with multiple booleans instead of a dedicated enum.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Boolean State Machine matters because the pattern here, State structs encoded with multiple booleans instead of a dedicated enum, shows that the code is bypassing a clearer or safer default that readers usually expect to see.
rules.fix: For Rust Boolean State Machine in Rust, prefer a dedicated enum when the semantics match, and keep the expected or cheaper API choice as the default.

rules.label: Rust Builder without Validate
rules.description: Builders that expose build() without an obvious validation step.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Builder without Validate matters because the pattern here, Builders that expose build() without an obvious validation step, removes a guardrail or signal that reviewers expect before trusting the surrounding code.
rules.fix: For Rust Builder without Validate in Rust, add an obvious validation step or move the code to a path where that safeguard is guaranteed before the risky work happens.

rules.label: Rust Constructor Many Flags
rules.description: Constructor-like APIs that use multiple boolean flags.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Constructor Many Flags matters because the pattern here, Constructor-like APIs that use multiple boolean flags, concentrates too much responsibility into one shape and makes future change riskier.
rules.fix: For Rust Constructor Many Flags in Rust, split the class or constructor work so one object owns one role and collaborators are injected or composed deliberately.

rules.label: Rust Global Lock State
rules.description: Static or lazy global state wrapped in mutable lock-based containers.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Global Lock State matters because the pattern here, Static or lazy global state wrapped in mutable lock-based containers, is worth review because good ownership and type contracts are a large part of what keeps Rust code ergonomic and safe.
rules.fix: For Rust Global Lock State in Rust, use the type system to make the contract obvious, and avoid APIs that force callers to remember hidden modes or invalid states.

rules.label: Rust Mutex Wrapped Collection
rules.description: Collection-plus-lock fields embedded directly in public or central state structs.

rules.label: Rust Option Bag Config
rules.description: Config-like structs with many Option fields and no obvious validation path.
rules.explanation: In Rust, Rust Option Bag Config matters because the pattern here, Config-like structs with many Option fields and no obvious validation path, pushes configuration or startup ownership into the wrong phase of the program.
rules.fix: For Rust Option Bag Config in Rust, load, validate, and wire this configuration at startup so request paths and imports only consume already-owned state.

rules.fix: For Rust Partial Init Escape in Rust, split the class or constructor work so one object owns one role and collaborators are injected or composed deliberately.
